retrieved_docs.title: The Effect of Pupil Size on Visual Resolution -- Clinical Significance -- Miotics
retrieved_docs.content: Pilocarpine: A parasympathomimetic alkaloid that constricts the pupil (miosis). Pilocarpine is used in conditions like glaucoma to increase the outflow of aqueous humor and reduce intraocular pressure. [109] Carbachol: A cholinergic drug that constricts the pupil. Carbachol is often used in the treatment of glaucoma to decrease intraocular pressure. [110] Alpha-2 agonists: Drugs like clonidine, used for high blood pressure, can cause pupil constriction. [111]

retrieved_docs.title: Prazosin -- Adverse Effects -- Serious Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Serious adverse effects include first-dose hypotension, and therefore it is recommended to take the initial dose at night. Also, patients may develop orthostatic hypotension and syncope. In cataract surgeries, patients taking prazosin may develop intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (IFIS) due to the drug's effect on the alpha receptors on the iris dilator muscle. However, some patients develop IFIS without using alpha-adrenergic receptor antagonists prior to surgery [12] .  Other serious adverse effects include priapism.

retrieved_docs.title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Equipment -- Adjunctive Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Some adjuncts have been used in conjunction with local anesthetics for improved effects and outcomes. Hyaluronidase is an enzyme widely used to enhance the spread of local anesthetic through the connective tissue. This, in turn, optimizes the rate of onset and surface area with preserved tissue contour; however, it may reduce the duration of the anesthetic effect. [17] Clonidine is an alpha2-adrenergic agonist used as a topical agent to reduce intraocular pressure, typically used in glaucoma cases, and provides enhanced intraoperative and postoperative analgesia when used in conjunction with an injected local anesthetic. Adverse effects of hypotension or excessive sedation are rarely encountered at the low utilized dose of 1mcg/kg. Dexmedetomidine is another alpha2-adrenergic agonist that may be used. [18] Using sodium bicarbonate for alkalinization of local anesthetics has been used to decrease the discomfort associated with the initial injection of the anesthetic and accelerate the onset of effect. Other adjuncts include epinephrine, muscle relaxants, opioids, and warming the local anesthetics. Many adjunctive agents have fallen out of favor due to associated risks. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Autonomic Pharmacology -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine: In excess, atropine can cause vision disturbances, resulting in prolonged mydriasis and cycloplegia. This drug can also exacerbate closed-angle glaucoma by increasing intraocular pressure. Reversal generally involves discontinuation; however, physostigmine can be used in extreme cases, such as severe elevation of body temperature and rapid supraventricular tachycardia. Clonidine: Excessive clonidine use can lead to xerostomia and sedation. Currently, there is no approved reversal agent. However, studies are underway investigating the use of naloxone as a potential reversal agent. β-Blockers: In addition to severe hypotension and bradycardia, tremors and bronchospasm are worrisome in cases of overdose. Glucagon serves as the reversal agent in such situations.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Hypotony -- Etiology -- Systemic Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors acetazolamide and methazolamide Antihypertensive drugs, e.g., clonidine, beta-blockers Nitrates Antivirals like Cidofovir Hyperosmotic agents, e.g., mannitol

retrieved_docs.title: Sympathetic Ophthalmia -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Glaucoma specialist: These specialists manage increased intraocular pressure secondary to or a side effect of corticosteroid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Prazosin -- Adverse Effects -- Serious Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: First dose hypotension Orthostatic hypotension Intraoperative Floppy iris syndrome Priapism Syncope

retrieved_docs.title: Amoxapine -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Angle-closure glaucoma: Amoxapine's potential for inducing pupillary dilation is a crucial concern, especially for patients with anatomically narrow angles, as it heightens the risk of angle-closure glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Prazosin -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Previous anaphylaxis with prazosin is an absolute contraindication. Otherwise, there are no absolute contraindications of this medication, and there are no black box warnings. As with all medications, the clinician should avoid prescribing prazosin if there is a hypersensitivity or prior adverse effect with other medications in this class (including terazosin, tamsulosin, doxazosin). Caution should be used in patients with hypotension due to the potential adverse effect of orthostatic hypotension, especially considering the first-dose phenomenon associated with the initiation of therapy with prazosin. Caution is also advisable in the setting of cataracts as antagonism of the alpha-1 receptors on the iris dilator muscle can contribute to floppy iris syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Glaucoma (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Normal-tension glaucoma can be treated with medications to lower intraocular pressure and should also have any other possible underlying medical conditions treated. Medications such as prostaglandin analogs, alpha-2 agonists, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors and miotics. Beta-blockers are controversial due to questions of decreased optic nerve head perfusion, especially with the potential exacerbation of the early AM nadir in blood pressure observed. If medical management fails, laser trabeculoplasty or filtration surgery can be utilized if continued progression of vision loss. In the Collaborative normal tension glaucoma study, patients with normal-tension glaucoma have been shown to slow or stabilize their field loss after a 30% reduction in IOP. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Isoproterenol -- Adverse Effects -- Ophthalmic
retrieved_docs.content: Blurred vision

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Tension Glaucoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Nocturnal hypotension – It is hypothesized that this causes reduced perfusion pressure at the optic nerve head. A 24-hour blood pressure analysis and avoiding nighttime dosing of systemic anti-hypertensives can be considered. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Congenital Glaucoma -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Brimonidine : This alpha-adrenergic agonist is contraindicated for children younger than 2 years of age because of the potential for central nervous system depression. Brimonidine needs to be used with caution in children younger than 6 years, as it can cross the blood-brain barrier and may cause respiratory depression, apnea, and drowsiness. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Receptor Agonists -- Indications -- Apraclonidine
retrieved_docs.content: Indications: Elevated intraocular pressure and as a diagnostic tool for Horner syndrome Administration: Eye drops Notes: Apraclonidine is also called iopidine. Often the elevated intraocular pressure that necessitates its use is caused by glaucoma. This drug is primarily an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist but does also produce alpha-1 agonist effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Protriptyline -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperthyroidism, urinary retention, and angle-closure glaucoma merit closer monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Ophthalmic sequelae
retrieved_docs.content: Glaucoma (angle recession or developmental) Detection: IOP measurement and gonioscopy should be performed every 6 months. Management: Topical aqueous suppressants (eg, timolol and dorzolamide) can be used; prostaglandin analogs may be added if tolerated. Surgical interventions include trabeculectomy or tube shunt placement to manage uncontrolled pressures. Progressive cataract formation Detection: Slit-lamp examinations are recommended annually. Management: Phacoemulsification with intraocular lens implantation should be performed once the cataract reduces visual acuity to worse than 6/12 or interferes with amblyopia therapy in children. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: Beta blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the drugs betaxolol and metipranolol. Both betaxolol and metipranolol are beta‑adrenergic antagonists that are employed as antiglaucoma agents and miotics, helping to lower intraocular pressure by decreasing aqueous humor production. As members of this same therapeutic group, they share similar mechanisms of action and are used in the management of ocular hypertension and glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1123
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Parasympathomimetics, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics” encompasses several agents that act on the parasympathetic nervous system to lower intra‑ocular pressure, including aceclidine, fysostigmin (physostigmine) and isofluorofát. Aceclidine is specifically classified as a miotic, indicating its primary action of inducing pupil constriction. This pharmacologic group is linked to neostigmin, a cholinesterase‑inhibiting miotic, reflecting shared mechanisms of enhancing cholinergic activity in the eye. Together, these compounds illustrate the relationship between parasympathomimetic drugs, antiglaucoma preparations, and miotics, highlighting their collective role in glaucoma management through miosis and reduction of intra‑ocular pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1960
retrieved_docs.content: Fysostigmin and isofluorofát are classified as members of the pharmacological group “Parasympathomimetics, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics.” This category comprises agents that mimic parasympathetic activity, are employed in the treatment of glaucoma, and produce miosis (pupil constriction). By acting as cholinergic agonists or acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, these drugs increase aqueous humor outflow and lower intra‑ocular pressure, thereby helping to manage glaucomatous optic neuropathy. Recognizing fysostigmin and isofluorofát as part of this class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies for patients requiring parasympathomimetic or miotic effects, particularly in ocular conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1265
retrieved_docs.content: Betaxolol and metipranolol are both members of the pharmacologic class described as “Beta blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics.” This class is itself defined as a type of antiglaucoma preparation and miotic, indicating that its constituent agents combine beta‑adrenergic blockade with ocular hypotensive and pupil‑constricting effects. Consequently, betaxolol and metipranolol are categorized as beta‑blocking agents that are also used as antiglaucoma medications and miotics, reflecting their role in reducing intra‑ocular pressure and managing glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1457
retrieved_docs.content: Levobetaxolol is a pharmacologic agent that may be used to treat فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني (ocular hypertension), helping to lower elevated intra‑ocular pressure and thereby reducing the risk of glaucoma‑related optic nerve damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 553
retrieved_docs.content: Levobetaxolol and levobetaxolol hydrochloride are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني (ocular hypertension). Both the base compound and its hydrochloride salt function to lower intraocular pressure, thereby contributing to the management of elevated eye pressure and related conditions such as glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1872
retrieved_docs.content: Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy are classified as antiglaucoma preparations and miotics, and they encompass specific agents such as dipivefrin and apraclonidine. These sympathomimetic drugs are employed to lower intra‑ocular pressure in patients with glaucoma, functioning within the broader category of antiglaucoma and miotic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1940
retrieved_docs.content: Parasympathomimetics, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics are classified as a subset of antiglaucoma preparations and miotics, representing a pharmacologic group that utilizes parasympathomimetic mechanisms to lower intra‑ocular pressure in glaucoma. Within this class, the agents fysostigmin and isofluorofát are recognized members, both acting as miotic agents that enhance aqueous humor outflow by stimulating the parasympathetic innervation of the eye. Their inclusion in this category underscores their shared therapeutic role in managing glaucoma through miosis and intra‑ocular pressure reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine and cyclopentolate are both classified as mydriatics, meaning they are pharmacologic agents used to dilate the pupil. As mydriatic drugs, they act on the iris muscles to produce pupil enlargement, which is valuable in ophthalmic examinations and certain therapeutic procedures. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and similar clinical applications in eye care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1357
retrieved_docs.content: Aceclidine is classified as a miotic agent (miotica), meaning it belongs to the drug group that induces pupil constriction. The miotic class of drugs is pharmacologically related to sympatholytic agents (sympatolytika), indicating that these two categories of medications share therapeutic connections, such as complementary or opposing effects on autonomic ocular regulation. Understanding that aceclidine is a miotic and that miotics are linked to sympatholytics helps clinicians consider drug interactions and combined effects when managing conditions affecting pupil size and intra‑ocular pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 496
retrieved_docs.content: Parasympathomimetics, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics is a pharmacological group that includes aceclidine, fysostigmin and isofluorofát. Within this group, aceclidine is classified as a miotic (miotika). Miotics are pharmacologically related to sympatholytics (sympatolytika), reflecting a shared impact on autonomic regulation. Recognizing that aceclidine, alongside other members such as fysostigmin and isofluorofát, belongs to this class helps clinicians understand their mechanisms for reducing intra‑ocular pressure and modulating parasympathetic activity, while also appreciating their connection to agents that inhibit sympathetic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1375
retrieved_docs.content: Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy comprise agents such as dipivefrin and apraclonidine, both of which are classified as members of this therapeutic class and are used to lower intraocular pressure in patients with glaucoma by stimulating adrenergic receptors and facilitating aqueous humor outflow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 460
retrieved_docs.content: The pharmacologic group identified as “Parasympathomimetics, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics” comprises several agents, including aceclidine, fysostigmin, isofluorofát, and neostigmin. Aceclidine is specifically classified as a miotic (miotika), indicating that it functions to constrict the pupil and lower intra‑ocular pressure. This miotic classification places aceclidine, along with other miotics, in a therapeutic relationship with sympatholytics (sympatolytika), reflecting the complementary or interacting roles of drugs that diminish sympathetic activity and those that enhance parasympathetic effects in ocular therapy. Collectively, these agents share the common mechanism of parasympathomimetic activity used in the management of glaucoma, and their inclusion in the same drug class underscores their related pharmacologic actions and relevance to patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2542
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIGLAUCOMA PREPARATIONS AND MIOTICS represent a broad category of ocular drugs used to lower intra‑ocular pressure, and within this category the subclass known as Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy is specifically recognized. Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy are therefore considered both a type of antiglaucoma preparation and miotic agent and a component of the larger antiglaucoma drug group. This subclass includes the agents dipivefrin and apraclonidine, both of which act via adrenergic mechanisms to reduce aqueous humor production or increase outflow, thereby contributing to intra‑ocular pressure control. The inclusion of dipivefrin and apraclonidine within Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy underscores their role as integral members of the overall antiglaucoma therapeutic arsenal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1347
retrieved_docs.content: Other antiglaucoma preparations in ATC is classified as a type of antiglaucoma preparations and miotics, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of medications used to lower intra‑ocular pressure and induce miosis in the management of glaucoma. Within this category, specific agents such as guanethidine and netarsudil are included as members, reflecting their role as distinct drug substances that share the therapeutic purpose of the class. Recognizing that guanethidine and netarsudil are part of the “Other antiglaucoma preparations in ATC” group helps clinicians understand the range of pharmacologic options available for glaucoma treatment and facilitates appropriate selection and categorization of these agents in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1878
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIGLAUCOMA PREPARATIONS AND MIOTICS is classified as a type of Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy, indicating that this broader drug group includes agents that act as sympathomimetics to treat glaucoma. Apraclonidine is identified as a member of the Sympathomimetics in glaucoma therapy class, meaning that apraclonidine functions as a sympathomimetic antiglaucoma preparation and miotic, used to lower intra‑ocular pressure in patients with glaucoma.
